title: esus’ first recorded prayer part 1
scripture_text: ‘’At that time Jesus said, “I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children. Yes, Father, for this is what you were pleased to do. All things have been committed to me by my Father. No one knows the Son except the Father, and no one knows the Father except the Son and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal him. “Come to me all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. for my yoke is easy and my burden is light.”
devotional:
This section is introduced by one of the few-recorded prayers of the Lord Jesus.

If we are going to learn anything from this prayer we must remember the context in which it was prayed. Three things had happened to Jesus that may have caused him to be discouraged.

a. John was in prison and because of his circumstances he began to doubt if he had made a mistake about Jesus, was He really the promised Messiah?

b. The generation in which Jesus lived was totally inconsistent. They condemned John for fasting, and condemned Jesus for feasting. It was a generation that could never be satisfied.

c. After months of ministry in the cities of Korazin, Bethsaida and Capernaum, Jesus who must have been weary had no choice than to denounce them for their unreasonable and determined rejection. This was not easy for Jesus, he loved these people, he had made his home with them. These denouncements most have taken their toll on him. So in order to keep his perspective, and to encourage him to reach out in the future, he turns to his Father in prayer.

What can we learn from this prayer?

When you consider the prayer life of Jesus as it is recorded in the four Gospels, there are two interesting factors. We are only told the contents of Jesus' prayers in the second half of his ministry. We have instances of Jesus praying in the first half of his ministry, but we are never told what he said. The reason for this may be that the Gospels writers are trying to teach us 'How we approach God is more important than what we say.'

The prayers that we do have recorded of Jesus, most of them are said in times of crisis, especially around his passion. Jesus did not allow the pain of life to prevent him from having an intimate relationship with his Father. Often in times of crisis our prayer life becomes more an expression of panic rather than fellowship. How we pray in times of difficulty reveals what we really think of God.

Jesus' relationship with his Father helped him cope with the stress of ministry.

I don't know if you have ever felt like a failure, sometimes feelings of failure are appropriate, you have an exam, or a driving tests, and you know that you have done nothing to prepare for it, and you fail, your not surprised. But what happens, when like Jesus, you give one hundred percent of yourself, hoping that people would turn to God and repent, but they don't. The feelings of failure would be far worse.

It would have been very easy for Jesus to give up, and think, "what's the point"? So to prevent discouragement from eating him up, he turned to his Father.

To call God, Father was a new concept to the Jews. In the Old Testament God is referred to as father, but only sparingly, only 21 times. And when he is referred to as father, it is never as 'father to an individual, but as father to the nation of Israel. However, in the gospels God is referred to as father 170 times. This was the title Jesus used most for God.

Jesus prayer was an expression of total submission.

We read "At that time Jesus said, "I praise you Father." When we think of praise, most of us think in terms of our emotions, we think that to praise God is to feel emotionally happy with whatever God brings into our life. This understanding is a perversion as the Greek word used here for praise is exomologeo. It means to acknowledge or agree fully: confess, profess, and promise. To praise God is to submit to him, to acknowledge that he God, and what he does is always right.

This kind of submission is only possible, when we are experiencing God as our Father, and we are living like his children. Surrendering our lives to God is not a one off experience, it is something that is ongoing, and is an essential requirement if we are going to cope with the demands of life. This kind of submission will produce a heart of gratitude, so we yield our lives to our Father as an expression of thanks and gratitude

Jesus prayer expressed the belief that his Father was sovereign.

'l praise you Father, Lord of heaven and earth."' Despite the rejection by those he loved, Jesus reminded himself that his father was still Lord. When we are under pressure we need to remind ourselves that God is Sovereign.

In prayer Jesus found comfort in the ways of the kingdom.

"l praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children." As Jesus communicated with his Father, he was aware that the ways of the Kingdom are very different to the ways of the world. The wise and the learned in this context were the Pharisees and the religious people who were privileged in many ways, yet they were obstinate and oblivious to the truth.

Jesus knew that his job was not to use intellectual persuasion or reasoned argument to convince people of his message. God resists the proud, and hides truth from them, but reveals truth to children. The word for children nepios literally means, "not speaking," so it refers to infants prior to their learning to speak. There is a very important principle here, the ways of kingdom are only learned, understood and applied properly in the context of a dependant relationship and ongoing communication with the father. The ways of the kingdom, and how God works in his world are not learned like a mathematical formula, but it is only as we open our hearts to God and depend on him, that we will be able to understand his ways. The truth that it was his Fathers pleasure to reveal these things...to little children" helped Jesus understand the rejection of the people in the three cities.

Jesus emphasises the importance of being a Son.

"All things have been committed to me by my Father. No one knows the Son except the Father, and no one knows the Father except the Son and those the son chooses to reveal him."

Fulfilling the role as a Son made it possible for Jesus to reach out to others in the future despite the rejection of the past. He remembered that being a Son was the most important thing, not being successful. As Jesus communicated with His father through this prayer, he was encouraged and strengthened, and he was able to make the invitation to the weary to come unto Him, not because success was guaranteed, but because he was feeling secure in His Fathers love.
prayer: Father help me to have Jesus’ attitude to prayer.
html:
esus’ first recorded prayer part 1

Matthew 11:25-30 J
‘’At that time Jesus said, “I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children. Yes, Father, for this is what you were pleased to do. All things have been committed to me by my Father. No one knows the Son except the Father, and no one knows the Father except the Son and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal him. “Come to me all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. for my yoke is easy and my burden is light.”

The wise and the learned in this context were the Pharisees and the religious people who were privileged in many ways, yet they were obstinate and oblivious to the truth.

The ways of the kingdom, and how God works in his world are not learned like a mathematical formula, but it is only as we open our hearts to God and depend on him, that we will be able to understand his ways. The truth that it was his Fathers pleasure to reveal these things...to little children" helped Jesus understand the rejection of the people in the three cities.

Jesus emphasises the importance of being a Son.

"All things have been committed to me by my Father. No one knows the Son except the Father, and no one knows the Father except the Son and those the son chooses to reveal him."

He remembered that being a Son was the most important thing, not being successful. Prayer: Father help me to have Jesus’ attitude to prayer.
